does: This paper applies sparse autoencoders to internal activations of language models to identify and extract interpretable features that address the problem of polysemanticity—where individual neurons activate across multiple semantically distinct contexts. By learning an overcomplete set of sparsely activating directions in activation space, the sparse autoencoders discover features that are more monosemantic and interpretable than neurons or other alternative methods. The authors validate interpretability using automated metrics and demonstrate that the learned features can be used to identify causally responsible features for specific model behaviors through counterfactual analysis, as shown in indirect object identification tasks.
connects: This work builds directly on efforts to understand neural network internals that have struggled with polysemanticity as a fundamental obstacle to interpretability. The paper engages with the superposition hypothesis—the theory that neural networks represent more features than they have neurons by exploiting high-dimensional geometry—which has emerged from recent work on neural network feature organization. Sparse autoencoders themselves extend prior work on using dimensionality reduction and sparse coding to disentangle learned representations. The paper also connects to broader mechanistic interpretability research that seeks causal explanations for model behaviors, building on techniques that probe internal representations for specific phenomena.
enables: This work opens pathways toward more comprehensive mechanistic understanding of large language models by providing a scalable method to extract and catalog interpretable features from neural activations. The discovered monosemantic features could enable more targeted interventions and modifications to model behavior, form the basis for adversarial robustness improvements through feature-level analysis, and support the development of better alignment techniques by clarifying what language models actually compute internally. The approach also potentially enables automated discovery of important features across different model layers and scales, which could facilitate studying emergence of capabilities and guide the design of more interpretable architectures.
